title: Heaps / Priority Queues
description: Master heap data structure and priority queue operations

documentation.content:
# Heaps & Priority Queues: Always Get the Best First

Imagine you're an emergency room doctor. Patients arrive constantly, but you can't treat them first-come-first-served—you need to prioritize by severity. The most critical patient always goes first, regardless of arrival time. That's exactly what a heap (priority queue) does—it always gives you the highest (or lowest) priority element instantly.

## The Story Behind Heaps

A heap is a special tree-based data structure that maintains a simple property: in a max-heap, every parent is larger than its children. In a min-heap, every parent is smaller than its children. This simple rule enables incredibly efficient priority-based operations.

Heaps are typically implemented as arrays (not pointer-based trees), using clever index math to navigate parent-child relationships.

## Two Types of Heaps

**Max-Heap** - Parent >= children. Root is the maximum element. Perfect for "get largest" operations.

**Min-Heap** - Parent <= children. Root is the minimum element. Perfect for "get smallest" operations.

## The Magic of Heap Operations

**Insert (Push)** - Add element at end, then bubble up: O(log n)
**Extract Max/Min (Pop)** - Remove root, move last element to root, bubble down: O(log n)
**Peek** - Look at root without removing: O(1)
**Heapify** - Build heap from array: O(n)

## When to Use Heaps

**Priority-based processing** - Always handle highest/lowest priority first
**K largest/smallest** - Maintain heap of size K
**Median finding** - Two heaps (max and min)
**Merge K sorted lists** - Min-heap with K elements
**Scheduling** - Task scheduling by priority
**Graph algorithms** - Dijkstra's, Prim's use heaps

## How It Actually Works

For array representation:
- Parent of index i: (i-1)/2
- Left child of i: 2*i + 1
- Right child of i: 2*i + 2

Max-heap example: [50, 30, 40, 10, 20, 15]
```
      50
     /  \
   30    40
  / \    /
 10  20 15
```

**Insert 45**:
1. Add at end: [50,30,40,10,20,15,45]
2. Bubble up: 45 > 40, swap → [50,30,45,10,20,15,40]

**Extract max**:
1. Remove 50, move 40 to root: [40,30,45,10,20,15]
2. Bubble down: 40 < 45, swap → [45,30,40,10,20,15]

## The Performance

**Insert**: O(log n)
**Extract**: O(log n)
**Peek**: O(1)
**Build heap**: O(n)
**Space**: O(n)

## Common Mistakes

❌ **Confusing max and min heaps** - Know which one you need!
❌ **Wrong parent/child formulas** - Remember: parent = (i-1)/2, left = 2i+1, right = 2i+2
❌ **Not maintaining heap property** - After insert/delete, must restore heap property
❌ **Using heap when array would work** - If no priority needed, array is simpler

## Pro Tips

💡 **Two heaps for median** - Max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half
💡 **Heap for Top K** - More efficient than sorting
💡 **Custom comparators** - Define priority for complex objects
💡 **Heapify is O(n)** - Building heap from array is faster than n inserts

## Real-World Applications

- **Operating systems** - Process scheduling, memory management
- **Dijkstra's algorithm** - Shortest path finding
- **Huffman coding** - Data compression
- **Event simulation** - Process events by timestamp
- **Load balancing** - Assign tasks to least loaded server
- **A* pathfinding** - Game AI, GPS navigation

Heaps are the go-to structure when you need "give me the best" operations repeatedly. They're efficient, elegant, and surprisingly simple once you understand the parent-child relationship! 🏔️✨

questions.question: In a max-heap, where is the largest element located?
questions.explanation: In a max-heap, the largest element is always at the root (index 0).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting an element into a heap?
questions.explanation: Insertion takes O(log n) time because we may need to bubble up through the height of the tree.

questions.question: For an element at index i in array representation, what is the index of its left child?
questions.explanation: In array representation, left child of index i is at 2*i + 1.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an unsorted array?
questions.explanation: Building a heap using heapify is O(n), which is more efficient than inserting n elements one by one.

problems.title: Kth Largest Element in Stream
problems.description:
Design a class to find the kth largest element in a stream. Note that it is the kth largest element in sorted order, not the kth distinct element.

Implement KthLargest class:
- KthLargest(int k, int[] nums) Initializes the object with integer k and stream of integers nums
- int add(int val) Appends integer val to the stream and returns the kth largest element
constraints: At most 10^4 calls will be made to add

exampleTestCases.description: Track 3rd largest as elements are added

problems.title: Last Stone Weight
problems.description:
You are given an array of integers stones where stones[i] is the weight of the ith stone.

We are playing a game with the stones. On each turn, we choose the heaviest two stones and smash them together. Suppose the heaviest two stones have weights x and y with x <= y. The result of this smash is:
- If x == y, both stones are destroyed
- If x != y, stone of weight x is destroyed, and stone of weight y has new weight y - x

At the end of the game, there is at most one stone left. Return the weight of the last remaining stone. If there are no stones left, return 0.

starterCode.python:
def last_stone_weight(stones):
    # Your code here
    # Use max-heap
    pass

solution.python:
import heapq

def last_stone_weight(stones):
    # Python heapq is min-heap, so negate values for max-heap
    heap = [-stone for stone in stones]
    heapq.heapify(heap)
    
    while len(heap) > 1:
        first = -heapq.heappop(heap)
        second = -heapq.heappop(heap)
        
        if first != second:
            heapq.heappush(heap, -(first - second))
    
    return -heap[0] if heap else 0

commonMistakes: Confusing max-heap and min-heap - know which one gives you the largest/smallest
Wrong parent/child index formulas - parent: (i-1)/2, left: 2i+1, right: 2i+2
Not maintaining heap property after operations - must bubble up/down
Using heap when simpler data structure would work
Forgetting that Python's heapq is min-heap by default - negate values for max-heap
patternDetection: Problem needs repeated 'get max' or 'get min' operations - heap is perfect
Finding Kth largest/smallest element - use heap of size K
Merging K sorted lists - min-heap with K elements
Median finding - two heaps (max for lower half, min for upper half)

walkthrough.title: Kth Largest Element in Array
walkthrough.description: Find the kth largest element using a min heap of size k for O(n log k) solution

steps.title: Initialize Min Heap of Size K
steps.explanation:
Create a min heap that will store exactly k elements.

Key insight: If we maintain the k largest elements in a min heap, the root (minimum of these k elements) is the kth largest overall!

Example: For [3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2, heap will contain [5,6], root=5 is 2nd largest.
steps.visualization:
nums = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k = 2

Goal: Keep 2 largest elements
Heap will be: [5,6]
Root (min of heap) = 5 = 2nd largest!
steps.insight: Using a MIN heap for LARGEST elements seems counterintuitive, but it's brilliant! The min element of the k largest is exactly the kth largest!

steps.title: Build Initial Heap
steps.explanation:
Add the first k elements to the heap.

For each addition, heapify up to maintain the min heap property (parent ≤ children).

After this, we have k elements in the heap.
steps.visualization:
nums = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2

Add 3: heap = [3]
Add 2: heap = [2,3]

Min heap property maintained!
Root = 2 (smallest of first 2 elements)
steps.insight: The first k elements give us our initial set of candidates. We'll improve this set as we see more elements!

steps.title: Process Remaining Elements
steps.explanation:
For each remaining element:
• If it's larger than the heap root (smallest of k largest), it belongs in our top k!
• Replace root with new element and heapify down
• If it's smaller, ignore it (not in top k)
steps.visualization:
heap = [2,3], processing 1:
1 < 2 (root), ignore

heap = [2,3], processing 5:
5 > 2 (root), replace!
heap = [3,5]

heap = [3,5], processing 6:
6 > 3 (root), replace!
heap = [5,6]
steps.insight: We only do O(log k) work per element, not O(log n)! This is why heap approach is efficient for large n and small k.

steps.title: Heapify Down After Replacement
steps.explanation:
When we replace the root, we might violate the min heap property.

Heapify down: Compare with children and swap with smaller child until heap property is restored.

This maintains our invariant: heap contains k largest elements.
steps.visualization:
After replacing root 3 with 6:

Before heapify:  After heapify:
      6              5
     / \            / \n    5   ?          6   ?

Heap property restored!
steps.insight: Heapify down is O(log k) because the heap height is log k. This is much better than sorting the entire array!

steps.title: Return Heap Root
steps.explanation:
After processing all elements, the heap contains the k largest elements.

The root (minimum of these k elements) is the kth largest element!

Time: O(n log k) - process n elements, each takes O(log k)
Space: O(k) - heap stores k elements
steps.visualization:
Final heap for [3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2:

heap = [5,6]
        ↑
      root

Return 5 (2nd largest element)

All elements: [1,2,3,4,5,6]
2nd largest: 5 ✓
steps.insight: This approach is optimal when k << n. For k=1, it's O(n). For k=n, sorting might be better. Choose based on k!
